project.title: Exosomal protein Profiling in Plasma of DOCA-Salt-Induced Hypertensive and Control Mice
project.projectDescription: This study aimed to profile exosomal protein extracted from plasma of male and female mice treated with DOCA-salt (a model for hypertension) and corresponding baseline controls. The isolated exosomal proteins were sequenced using using LC-MS/MS (Orbitrap Exploris 480+FAIMS Pro). This approach may help identify molecular mechanisms related to sex-specific hypertension responses.This study aimed to profile exosomal protein extracted from plasma of male and female mice treated with DOCA-salt (a model for hypertension) and corresponding baseline controls. The isolated exosomal proteins were sequenced using using LC-MS/MS (Orbitrap Exploris 480+FAIMS Pro). This approach may help identify molecular mechanisms related to sex-specific hypertension responses.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: Plasma-derived extracellular vesicles were first captured using magnetic beads, followed by protein extraction with a lysis buffer containing 7 M urea and 2% SDS supplemented with protease inhibitors. After centrifugation, protein concentration was measured using the BCA method. A defined amount of protein (30 µg) underwent reduction with dithiothreitol (DTT) and alkylation with iodoacetamide (IAA), followed by protein precipitation, acetone washes, and trypsin digestion. The resulting peptides were desalted using C18 columns and reconstituted in 0.1% formic acid. Samples were then analyzed by LC-MS/MS on an EASY-nLC 1200 system coupled to an Orbitrap Exploris 480 + FAIMS Pro mass spectrometer, using data-dependent acquisition with high-resolution full scans and HCD fragmentation. Data processing and protein identification were performed with Proteome Discoverer 2.4 against the SwissProt Mus musculus database
project.dataProcessingProtocol: Raw MS/MS data generated from the Orbitrap Exploris 480 + FAIMS Pro were processed using Proteome Discoverer 2.4 (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The analysis workflow involved spectrum matching against the SwissProt Mus musculus database using trypsin as the digestion enzyme, allowing up to two missed cleavages. Carbamidomethylation (C) was set as a fixed modification, while oxidation (M) and protein N-terminal acetylation were included as variable modifications. The precursor mass tolerance was set to 10 ppm, and peptide length was limited to ≥6 amino acids. False discovery rate (FDR) thresholds were applied at both the peptide and protein levels, with a strict FDR of 1% (0.01) and a relaxed cutoff of 5% (0.05). For quantification, a label-free quantification (LFQ) approach was employed based on precursor ion intensity. Protein abundance values were normalized across samples, and pairwise comparisons between experimental groups were performed. Differentially expressed proteins were identified using two main criteria: (1) fold change ≥1.2 or ≤0.83, and (2) supported by ≥2 unique peptides. Identified proteins were subsequently subjected to functional annotation, including GO enrichment (biological process, molecular function, and cellular component) and KEGG pathway analysis, with significantly enriched terms visualized using barplots and dotplots.
